content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 《非诚勿扰》，大家好，我是杜素娟，是在华东政法大学教文学课的老师。其实呢，这个身份挺尴尬的，每次我一介绍我这个身份啊，总是会有人跑上来说：“政法大学的老师啊，你给我来一点那个法律咨询呗！”其实我在华东政法大学啊，教了二十五年的文学课，成功地把自己变成了一个华东政法大学的著名“法盲”。所以在华东政法大学呢，我们所有的专业分两类，一类叫法学专业，还有一类呢叫非法学。那像我这种中文专业的老师，一般都会自称为“非法”的老师。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 刚进大学的第一天，刚进宿舍，相互之间都还不认识。然后我一进去，发现我对面的床板上坐着一个人——我们那时候才十八岁嘛，但他看上去已经有将近四十岁的样子，一脸沧桑，戴着个眼镜，是我的老乡，山东栖霞。结果一聊天一问，他是一九九四年当年高考山东省的文科状元，山东省的第一名。然后他们村从来没有出过大学生，别说北大的学生了。所以那一年他考了山东省文科状元以后，他们县的县长开着县里最豪华的桑塔纳轿车，一路把他送到北京。当时他们村都轰动了，十里八乡的都来送他，那些大娘都拿着鸡蛋说：“孩子，要活着回来啊！”大娘们还以为是当年抗日战争送小伙子上前线的感觉。他跟我们讲这些的时候，我们当时真的乐坏了。那个时候，可能对于他们村子里来讲，一代人的梦想就是能培养出一个大学生。然后他很争气，考了山东省第一名，进了北大。从那一天开始，我相信他就在自己的心里，为自己四年以后做了最充分的安排和准备。从那一天开始，我相信他就在自己的心里，为自己四年以后做了最充分的安排和准备。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 哈喽，大家好，我是刘恋，是一名音乐唱作人，也曾经是一名广告创意人。从业以来，将近十二年的时间里，我一直从事着两份职业：白天在广告公司，像今天这样跟PPT打交道；晚上就和音乐、舞台打交道，算是一个相对典型的“斜杠青年”吧。我在三十二岁的时候，因为参加《乘风破浪的姐姐》，有了机会成为一名全职的音乐人，成了所谓“打工人勇闯娱乐圈”的一名中年插班生。当我知道今天的主题是“在场”的时候，我觉得这也太巧了——因为我在31岁的时候，写过一首生日歌，那首歌就叫做《在场》。在“斜杠”那些年里，我做过最有仪式感的一件事，就是每年过生日的时候，会给自己写一首歌。身旁人会说：“哇塞，你不愧是做音乐的人，好浪漫啊！”但其实我一开始写这些歌的初衷，源于我对自身存在与价值的焦虑。我第一次决定给自己写歌，是在27岁快过生日的前两天。那晚，我刚加完班，坐着地铁回家，一边听着歌，一边看着窗外发愣，突然产生了一种前所未有的恐惧感。作为一个曾经的重度文艺青年，我所喜欢的那些音乐人，像Kurt Cobain、Amy Winehouse，他们都是“二十七岁俱乐部”的成员。他们在27岁时，已经留下了无数经典作品，然后将生命以非常激烈的方式定格在了二十七岁。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 大家好，我是段睿，蔡磊“渐冻症”一战的负责人，京东前副总裁蔡磊的妻子，“渐冻症”资金的资助者。大家知道我们的故事，或许是从我先生罹患“渐冻症”开始，或许是从我们为“渐冻症”投入上亿资金开始。悲情、温柔、知性、贤惠、隐忍、坚强，我被以各种温顺的辞藻形容和赞美。不知道从何时开始，“温柔”“贤惠”成为了我的专属，但大家是否知晓，这个世界其实很现实？我们缺乏巨大的资金支持，一个亿的投入——一个亿，是温柔和贤惠远远换不到的。记者经常问我，为何选择在这场战役中加入进来？为何要选择在场？我说，作为女性，我们有很多角色：母亲、女儿、妻子。自古以来，我们从未离场，只是似乎总以若有若无的背景音、背景色，被这个世界提及到。六年了，我总是被问，我先生被确诊时的情景是怎样的？我知道大家期待看到我的泪目。我总是被提及，我是否被迫走出家门抛头露面。我总是被问，是何等的爱，让我放弃事业，辅助先生的事业？我总是被问，我是否后悔当时结束了自己的审计生涯？我想说，如果我是男性，如果是我妻子罹患重病，你们是否还会有这样的疑问？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 村上春树说：“普通人啊，生在普通家庭，长在普通家庭，一张普通的脸，普通的成绩，想着普通的事情。”我就是这样一个普通的人。我出生于河北省东部的一个小小乡村，在那里度过了平平淡淡将近十年的时光。小镇不大，但傍晚的风总是很温柔，人也憨厚。我像大多数农村的孩子一样，白天和小伙伴一起奔跑，一起爬树，一起在麦地里打滚；到了傍晚，就搬着小凳子坐在自家门口，吹着凉风，摇着爷爷的蒲扇。那时候，因为简单，所以快乐。我由农村到城市上学的契机，其实是乡下教育资源的匮乏。两间屋子，三个年级，这就是我当时上学的地方。你们可能这辈子都没有经历过老师向学生请教单词发音这样奇葩的事情，因为我们的英语老师都是语文老师客串的，他们可能上午听过几遍录音，下午就来给我们上课了。于是，我离开了家，离开了那片给我生命、让我成长的土地，到了城里读书。我最初的愿望其实是很美好的：明亮的教室，统一的书桌，辽阔的操场，还有那早就听说过的神奇的多媒体教学。可等我真正到了那里才发现，事情远非那么简单。我至今仍然清楚地记得，新学期报到的那一天，我用家乡话做出的自我介绍，惹得全班同学哄堂大笑。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 孩子们，你们好，我是范伟。最近毕业季，有的同学问我，说要毕业了，不知道该选哪条路。我想，路这个事吧，没有标准答案，但我倒可以跟你们唠唠我的路。刚入行学相声的时候，有人说：“你的口音太重，不适合走这条路，还学吗？”哎呀，我这心里就犹豫了：万一学不成怎么办？荒废了大好时光，最后的结果是个笑话，怎么办？但我不想退，因为我喜欢。我也不想停，一旦停下来，心里那个犹豫就像野草一样，越长越高，把我那个喜欢全给遮上了。所以，我想走走试试。当我在这条路上越走越顺的时候，我记着有一位前辈跟我说：“你小子天生是吃这碗饭的。”所以，甭管哪条路，只要你喜欢，咱先走两步儿。说到这，该有人问了：“万一走错了咋整？”

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 别立福，一个相貌普通的中年男人，做着一份普通的刑警工作，大家都喜欢叫他老别。普通的老别有个不普通的特长——跑得快，他追小偷能把小偷累趴下。他干反扒七年，小偷抓了三百多个，案子破了六百多起。他先参军，后从警，荣立二等功两次、三等功九次、嘉奖十二次。老别不仅把自己跑成了超级警察，还跑成了光荣的奥运火炬手。所以啊，长相普通的老别一点不普通，他是老百姓眼里的守护神，是同事们学习的好榜样，是女儿心中的英雄。老别是七二年生人，他要是还活着，今年就整五十了。是的，老别不在了，算起来他已经走了四年了。二零一九年的一月十九号，其实那天老别已经下班了，但接到了搜捕任务的通知，他毫不犹豫地就去了。凌晨时分，老别带着几个年轻队员，打着手电，深一脚浅一脚地仔细搜索着。突然，一个黑影跳了出来，猛地朝走在最前面的老别扑了过去。“在这儿有洞！”就在老别提醒战友的同时，刀尖已经扎进了他的胸膛。老别他不是不能跑啊，可他要是跑开了，身后的战友就危险了。他不仅没有跑，反而一脚把歹徒踹倒在地，死死地抱住了他。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 那第二个感受呢，就是不管你做的事情如何，做好多么充分的准备，即使在完全没有变量的前提之下，也会有改变，而这些改变带来的结果是比较深刻的。比如说，我大概都知道今天来演讲的人啊，他们都会讲什么内容，结果发现上台来演讲的嘉宾，大部分都没有按自己原来的稿子讲。为什么呢？当他们一下面对灯光和观众的时候，要不就是忘了，要不就是想到了别的故事。其实这很能象征我们的人生，不管我们做多少准备，因为这个世界在变化，你周围的人在变化，你周围人的想法在变化，你自己也在变化。所以呢，你的准备再充分，也必须面对每时每刻的改变，而只有你充分地做好应对这些改变的准备以后，你才能应对一切场面。所以像我就比较明智，从来没有准备任何稿子，因为我知道准备了也没用。那么，在我前一段时间说了不端的女性言论以后呢，其实我拒绝了。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我叫罗翔，有很多种身份，我的主业呢，是在中国政法大学当老师，当然还有一个身份，大家可能也比较熟悉，就是B站的一个UP主。我今天啊，想给大家分享的主题是如何做一个称职的老师。当然，在分享这个主题的时候，可能首先要假定自己是一位称职的老师，但说实话，我的内心并没有这种笃定。虽然在从教生涯中获得过一些荣誉，比如我自己非常看重的，那就是在我们学校曾经多次入选最受本科生欢迎的十位教师，但说实话，我时常怀疑，我到底是不是一位称职的教师呢？所以我今天想和大家一起来探讨，从事一份职业到底怎样才算称职。我想给大家分享我自己的三个时刻。第一，我是如何当上老师。其实我从1999年在政法大学上研究生的时候就开始讲课，那个时候是讲自考，目的很明确，就是为了赚钱，为了养活自己，为了减轻父母的负担。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我是小何，何运晨。首先呢，要跟大家表达一下我今天的喜悦之情，因为今天我是以毕业生的身份站在这里的。毕竟已经快三十的人了，“毕业生”这三个字我真的很珍惜。首先从事实层面来叙述，我最后一次从校园中毕业已经是三年之前的事情了，但是从另外一个维度来讲，每过一段时间，我们的人生都需要一次总结和告别，在我看来这也是一种毕业，而今天我就是以这样一种毕业生的身份，和大家分享一些我自己的小故事和想法。现在还在学校的朋友们，大家会不会觉得“毕业季”这三个字听起来超级浪漫，就充满了那些不舍、告别、成长和泪水？但是只要你经历过毕业，你就会晓得，上面那些词哈，基本上是对于那些已经找到工作的人来说的，而对于那些暂时还没有找到工作的同学来说，其实只有一个词，那就是焦虑。三年之前我可太焦虑了，听到这儿，有的朋友可能会说：“小何凡尔赛了是不是？你不是拿到那个心动的offer了吗？”不瞒你说，offer挺心动的，但是当时其实来的没有那么及时。我还记得那个时候，应该是离毕业还有大半年的时间，当时同学之间讨论最多的话题，其实就是“隔壁寝室的谁谁谁拿到了哪里的offer”。那我当时手里啥也没有呀，我心想，竟然还有我插不进去的话题，不行，我也要聊这个话题。于是我海投简历，投出去大概有几百份吧，什么HR的公共邮箱、合伙人的私人邮箱、各种各样的公众号、各种各样的求职群。我每天早上起来刷群里的消息，看看到底有没有什么合适的实习机会，终于有一天，我拿到了一个不错的实习机会。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我记得一百多年前张之洞先生写过一幅对联，叫做“数百年旧家无非积德，天下第一好事还是读书”。今天呢，我们的主题叫做“终身阅读”，那么读书这件事，它到底能给我们带来什么？这听起来或许是一个很宏大的命题，但今天呢，我想跟大家分享一点我个人化的感受。首先我认为，阅读能够带给我们一种目光。我出生在九十年代初的一座北方小城，那里有着中国最为丰富的煤炭资源，也因此呢，小城里面的产业结构相对单一，人们的生活节奏也很缓慢。但是就是在那一座小城里啊，我十岁之前的记忆都和它息息相关。我记得每到中午，整个小城会陷入一种相对的安静，因为大家都去午休了，大街上也没有什么行人。有一天中午呢，我搬了一把小小的竹椅子，坐在我姥姥家的窗前读书。那是我第一次读到李白的诗：“故人西辞黄鹤楼，烟花三月下扬州。孤帆远影碧空尽，唯见长江天际流。”有一束阳光穿过窗帘照在地上，风起帘动，那一刻我觉得真美。在那之前，我其实没有去过扬州，也没有去过黄鹤楼，但在那一刻，我感受到了一个词叫做“远方”。它是一种懵懂的期待，还有向往，让你想要翻过高山，去看看山的那边都有什么。我想，这就是我第一次在精神意义上走出那一座小城。后来我又去了很多地方，但每当我想到梦开始的那一秒，我总会想到这个午后。所以你说，读书这件事，它真的能够改变你的人生轨迹吗？真的。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我虽只二十八年岁月，却当了二十八年被爷爷视作春枝树的孩子，他用朴素的人生智慧教我要勇敢、要善良，用笨拙的守护为我撑起一片小天地。爷爷背着草药筐上山采药，顶着烈日种田的身影，是我小时候最深刻的画面。可即便有爷爷的爱，父母的缺席依然像一根刺。成长的路上，总有人带着偏见嘲笑，我们是不是都有过这样的时刻：被别人的标签困住，忍不住怀疑自己是不是真的不值得被期待？我看着爷爷为了供我读书起早贪黑的身影，总觉得是自己拖累了他，不止一次在深夜里抱着枕头哭，甚至冒出 “如果我不在了，爷爷是不是就能轻松点” 的绝望念头。但每次这个念头冒出来，爷爷的叮嘱就会在我耳边想起，他说：“你一定要好好活着，不然我的付出就白费了。” 我咬着牙告诉自己，原生家庭给的是起点，不是终点，别人的缺席不该成为自己放弃的理由。这是我第一次学会对抗被定义，也是毫无保留相信自己的最初萌芽。原来，别人怎么看，从来不能决定我们是谁。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Welcome to Ex Libris — a podcast about personal reflections and reviews on reading.A library, after all, is an act of pure generosity.Thank you for joining me in collecting yet another book.It’s September again — the start of a new academic year. Countless students are stepping onto university campuses, entering a wider, fresher world.For those who love reading, one name will sooner or later cross your path — Hermann Hesse.Freshmen, having just stepped out of the tension and narrowness of high school, are now feeling the shock of a new environment. They’re eager to experience different ways of living, to explore diverse worldviews — and that’s why Hesse speaks to them so deeply.His Demian has inspired countless young people lost in confusion. Hesse’s words are full of self-affirmation, challenge to rigid authority, and longing for a rich, meaningful solitude — qualities that make his writing both admirable and profoundly relatable.

theme: 《在轮下》：孤独的做题家，不要被优绩主义碾于车轮之下

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: So the English were willing to trade goods of military value with the Ottomans. Definitely keep that in mind as the story of this visit to Chios unfolds.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Chios is a major trading destination a couple of miles off the coast of Anatolia. It’s now a part of Greece and was almost certainly mostly Greek in character when Harbrown was hanging out there in early fifteen eighty one, partying with the French consul, considering a pilgrimage to Jerusalem, and deciding he'd change his surname to Harborne when he got home to make it sound more prestigious.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Across the water were the ports of Fouquet, or Fosa, and Smearin, or Ismir.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: It was a good place to be and undoubtedly a great place to do business. Unfortunately, he was not the only Englishman looking to go about business on the island at this time.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: A ship called the Bark Row, owned by the Newcastle-born merchant Roger Rowe, had left London in September fifteen eighty bound for the Mediterranean.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: It traded cargo at Livorno, Malta, and finally Chios. Cargo destined for Catholic ports had been standard English fare. The cargo destined for Ottoman Chios, however, was a shipment of broken LED church bells.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Harbourne and his Flemish business partner generously hosted the ship's captain at his lodgings with…

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: We really deeply loved each other. The way it ended was so pleasant that I think I can talk about it without crying right now. It’s only been a week, and I can talk about it because it was mutual. We communicated, got closure, and decided to remain friends.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: It was as perfect a breakup as possible, but it’s still tough. So, what does this mean for me? Well, now I’m single, and being single is interesting because I haven’t been single much in my life since I was seventeen.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: I’ve dated pretty consistently, with a few boyfriends, and very short breaks in between, so I haven’t really been single. It’s a little daunting to be in this place again. This time, I know I should be single longer than just two or three months — maybe for a year. I feel it in my gut, and I know it’s right, but it’s daunting.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: I don’t know what it is about me, but I just like thinking about a cute boy all the time. I always need some sort of crush — a celebrity, a random barista, anything. I’m the type of person who always has to have a crush.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: That leads me to dating a lot, because once I fixate on a crush and they like me back, I feel like I have to date them since I’ve had the crush for a while. So, I think I date often because I’m constantly having crushes.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Also, I don’t enjoy casual hookups much. If I want to be physical with a guy, I prefer to be in a relationship. I’m not particularly driven by sex.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: But I don’t think it was helping me grow my following. The more click-baity videos — like “I crocheted a six hundred dollars top” or “I’m in a sweater in a day” — are the ones that end up on someone’s homepage or suggested videos and bring new people into the community.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Especially when I first started doing content full time earlier this year, I was really focused on scaling and making this a sustainable income as quickly as possible.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: To be transparent, things haven’t scaled as I hoped. You’ve probably noticed most of my videos have ads now, but it hasn’t grown at the rate I wanted. The reason I’m bringing the podcast back isn’t because I make so much money that I don’t need to grow my following — it’s that I’ve dissociated from the financial aspect because it stresses me out too much.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: I’m trying to be more intuitive and post what I feel like posting, while also considering the content you all want to see.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: I mean, how often do you hear stories about royal servants? Almost never, except for Francois. History often mistakes Francois for being a chef himself, but that was never the case — he just handled everything in the front of house.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Anyway, this story begins and ends all the way back in sixteen seventy one in fancy-pants France. At this time, France was experiencing a golden age under Supreme French King Overlord Louis the forteenth — history’s longest-reigning monarch to date, ruling for over senvety two and a half years.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Under his rule, French power expanded across the world, arts and culture flourished, and the wealthy lived in utmost extravagance. Sure, there were plenty of wars in the background, but that was for the poor people to worry about.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: With such indulgence pouring out of every orifice, parties, galas, celebrations, shindigs, and balls were just the norm. But these royal retreats demanded a professional to ensure everyone kept a crusty yellow smile on their dusty white faces. That’s where Francois came in.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 欢迎收听《藏书票》。这是一档关于个人阅读感想与评论的播客节目。一座图书馆就是毫无保留的慷慨。感谢你和我一起收藏一本书。又到了九月份，又是一个开学季。不计其数的学生们走进大学校园，迎来崭新的、广阔的世界。对于其中喜欢阅读的朋友们来说，赫尔曼·黑塞或许是你早晚都会见到的名字。大一新生们刚刚走出高中的紧张与逼仄，正感受着生活环境的剧烈变化，正渴望着体验到其他的生活方式，渴望着领略丰富多彩的世界观。这使他们更容易被黑塞所吸引。他笔下的德米安不知激励了多少迷茫的少年，黑塞的文字里充满了对自我的肯定，对固化的权威思维的挑战，对富有内涵的高质量孤独的向往，既令人钦佩又觉得亲切。

theme: 《在轮下》：孤独的做题家，不要被优绩主义碾于车轮之下

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 第一次是二零一一年，是一个美国人，嗯，然后其实也是因为这次飞行，给我心里埋下一个种子，嗯，然后我就开始学习翼装飞行。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 第二次，呃，穿越天空洞是我做的，然后是二零二三年。嗯，那也很近的事情。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 哈，对，就是两年前。二零一一年是一个美国人。对，是个美国人，然后也是翼装飞行这样的运动，呃，第一次进入中国就做了一个这样的挑战。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 那您在学翼装飞行之前是玩什么的呀？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我是飞滑翔伞啊，也是一种飞行运动啊。然后我简单介绍一下，我是咱们国家第一个滑翔伞的世界冠军啊，这是2009年在克罗地亚，然后从这以后呢，我就开始……

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我还行，就我大部分的时候都还是乐呵呵的，就基本上跟台上的状态是一样的，忙忙叨叨忙忙叨叨，然后乐乐呵呵，然后也没时间想这些东西。偶尔空下来之后我也会去想说，诶，就让大家乐这事儿这么重要，这个大家这么需要吗？就偶尔也会这么想，但也想不出来，就说行，你们说需要就需要吧，反正你们觉得开心我就继续讲。基本就这么一个状态，想也想不明白，你想不想，这一切有什么意义？这样的问题想也会想，但其实也是想不出一个答案。然后，你还不如用做去替代这个想和这个意义，反正做了可能就有点意义吧，我就是这么去说服自己。所以你看啊，我也不瞒大家，就是说我请这个赵琳老师来呀，专门从武大飞过来的，我有我的个人情结在里面。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 就是他曾经陈白露曾经美好的回忆那一段，然后呢，就因为他有合适的人一起私奔到乡下，然后还结了婚，还生了孩子，然后孩子又死了。对于我来说，刚毕业就是这些事情，感觉就是离我很遥远，虽然说也见过，但是真的从发自内心的感受上肯定还是有距离的。然后呢，远征老师那天就说，你来，你们俩做练习，你现在就想一个你心里刻骨铭心的一件事情。也不是事情，是情感，是刻骨铭心的情感经历。用陈白露的词对，就是用那段那段词去说，但你可能表达自己的内心。然后当时吧，也不知道就怎么在那个诡异的气氛下突然一下，因为我平时其实不算一个特别感性的人，突然的情感经历——就是失恋了是吗？情感经历也不是那么丰富，但好在还有刻骨铭心的部分，所以挺感谢那段经历的。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 实际上，它就是特定约束你走路的品味。举止的方向要端正，身子不能乱摆。所以这个在古代都有，就是整个我们的品位，实际上它就是生活长期惯性。实际上，品味的另一个概念就是“三个口味”。很多人说这件事儿就是品味有很多种，所以我的意思是说，所谓大家说你认可啊，所谓大家还是圈子，其实是有圈子的。像我们回看维多利亚年代也好，巴黎所谓闲阶级、资产阶级出来取代贵族的品味的时候，居然会有一句话，用中产阶级的字，跟资产阶级的那个品味圈形成的时候，居然瞧不起贵族的品味。我们经常说，哎呀，真贵族感觉是好东西是吧？他们瞧不起的时候，会说这个人的品味蛮贵族的嘛，就是说蛮贵族的庄重，用普通话可能是高大上等等哈。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 周教授和研究团队在国政中心的实验场，就像在玩超大型的积木一样，只是他们不是在盖房子喔，而是在“震”房子。他们用机器模拟地震，并且用 AI 监测量的受力状况观察，在强震下哪个部位会先撑不住？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 而就在这里诞生了一项超酷的新技术，叫做夹型钢骨挫曲数字消能协撑，简称 SBRB。你觉得听起来很拗口吗？没错，但这就是科学啊。简单讲就是让建筑物少摇一点的秘密武器，地震来的时候，SBRB 就像安全气囊一样，可以吸收能量，减少晃动。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 避免梁柱断裂。而且以前这种斜撑要灌混凝土又重又难施工。周教授的版本呢？完全不需要混凝土，是用全钢打造，耐震、好修，还能重复使用。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 他们的来源通常有三个。第一个是对疗程的不确定，就是要打多少针啊，到该请多少假啊？这是他们会焦虑的点。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 再来，其实一个蛮大的焦虑点就是如果有胚胎植入后，他在等怀孕的期间，其实这是压力最大的，因为这等待啊。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 第三个是那些反复的病人，比如植过一次失败，他第二次准备再做，甚至做到第三次。这个就叫反复着床失败，或者是反复流产。那这类的病人，反复失败的病人，最后可能都会转向去检查免疫啊，接受一些免疫治疗啊，可能就是为了越弄越焦虑，然后越失调，所以我觉得这是我。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 第一期P4，所以在糖尿病的新药研发中，有一种新药是第一批 P4。它的原理是，你只要抑制 DPP4，把它抑制掉的话，看 GLP-1 在体内能不能活得比较久一点。平常上 GLP-1 在身体里面时间非常短，打进去的话，几个小时就分解掉了。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我刚才提到的 No or No disc，我一直在做糖尿病的研究。那 DISC 跟 AID 一直在糖尿病药物领域竞争，在那个 purify 的领域里，两边差不多，一个是在欧洲，一个是在美洲。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 但是 human recombinant DNA 从此做的人类试验，这是在美国做的。那时候有一个生技公司叫 Genentech，但做出来之后就卖给了 Ella。它是 一九八三 年进行的人类药物试验。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 比如说，生理方面，其实卵子最大的敌人就是氧化压力。随着年龄的上升，我们体内的自由基就会累积增加。原因是随着年纪增长，我们的抗氧化酵素也会缺乏，所以造成 ROS 自由基的累积。自由基的累积可能会去攻击卵子里面的线粒体。线粒体就是卵子的发电机嘛，卵子如果没电，它就容易分裂出错。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 染色体就会分裂不完全，染色体异常，所以怀孕率下降、流产上升。这就是随着年龄变大导致的氧化压力问题。再来，随着年纪增长，我们的血流，卵巢的血流也会减少。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 所以荷尔蒙、卵巢生成的荷尔蒙功能就会变差，也会造成随着年龄增长，荷尔蒙分泌出现问题。这是生理的因素。基因方面的话，也可能是，大家都知道，随着老化，基因修复能力在退化。那么基因修复能力呢？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 大家想像一下，你手边呢，可能有一个看不见的小盒子，但这个很小很小的盒子里面，它的内部空间居然可以比一个足球场还要大。这个就是M,O,F非常神奇的地方。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 那M,O,F呢，其实就是一种充满孔洞的全新材料。你可以把它想像成微观世界里的乐高积木。科学家会利用金属离子当成枢纽，再用有机分子当作连接棒，把它们巧妙地连在一起。这样就会形成一个有规律、同时充满空腔的巨大晶体结构。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: M,O,F最厉害的地方，就是它内部巨大的表面积。透过改变分子的组成，科学家可以精准设计出具有特定功能的小分子结构，这些结构能容纳大量气体或化学物质。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 第一个提出M,O,F概念的人是 Robson。他在一九七四年提出了初步想法，但还没实际动手。一直到一九八九年，他才利用原子本身的吸引力，让分子能够自我组织。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 诶，但是今年的诺贝尔奖得主呢，却说“不对哦，宏观物体其实也有机会出现量子效应”。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 他们打造了一个特制的超导电路，让几十亿个电子手牵手，一起组成一个超大的量子团队。结果这群电子居然能像一个“单一粒子”一样，集体一起穿越——不是个人穿越，而是整群穿越。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 所以整个电路就像幽灵一样，从这边突然闪到那边。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 那这个研究为什么重要呢？因为它让我们真的看到：量子效应在非常巨量的情况下也能存在。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 更惊人的是，他们还发现这个电路吸收能量的方式“不是连续的”。连续的意思就是，能量变化像一条平滑的曲线。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 可是在这个电路里，能量吸收却像爬楼梯一样，一阶一阶往上跳。这就是量子物理一个非常基本的概念：能量量子化。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 原本只会出现在教科书、微观世界的概念，居然在相对巨大的系统中也能出现。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 当初复制出桃丽阳的方法，他们从人类的皮肤细胞里取出完整、含有四十六条染色体的细胞核。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 然后把它植入一个已经移除自身遗传物质的卵子里。但是问题来了，皮肤细胞有四十六条染色体，而正常的卵子应该只有 23 条染色体。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 这刻改造过的卵子有点像行李已经被操纵的旅客，因为它必须想办法丢掉其中一半的行李。为了解决这个问题，团队开发了一种技术，叫做有失减速分裂，对，就是有失分裂和减速分裂的结合。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 他们用一种叫螺丝科维丁的药物来“欺骗”卵子，让它以为自己正在进行正常分裂，就会把一半的染色体偷偷塞进一个小小的集体里，把它丢到外面去。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 用这个聪明的方法，他们成功把一个有四十六条染色体的细胞变成了一个只有二十三条染色体、还能与精子结合的功能性卵子。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 那不管是老师或是家长，有时候呃，你回答基本上有两个大方向。第一大方向是会导致压抑式的互动，另外一种就是鼓励式的互动。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 压抑式的互动，比如说有时候小朋友问问题，你会说，“欸，这个你问那么多干嘛？”

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 这有时候会传达出一种“提问是在制造麻烦”的讯号，会导致小朋友越来越不喜欢问问题。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 这种压抑式的语言还有一种可能性，比如说他们会说，“这个问题不重要，这个问题没用，所以不要胡思乱想。”这种回答方式就是把小朋友的思考分类成有用或没用、重要或不重要，会削弱他们的内在动机，因为把他们的问题变成一种应用式或者有没价值式的分类，会导致内在动机下降。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 还有另一种常见的压抑式回答，就是说，“诶，你长大就知道了，你现在还小，你不懂。”这种方法等于是把知识和权威绑在一起，也会削弱小孩子主动学习、想要学习的动机。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 从小就念生物学课本，课本只告诉我们，细胞里的线粒体是帮我们制造能量的“能量工厂”。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 但在看了科学杂志文章后我才知道，哦，原来线粒体还有这么多功能，而且我们是从妈妈这边遗传到线粒体的。所以这种线粒体遗传疾病，实际上是从妈妈这边过来的。像 LHON 这种病，以及很多线粒体 DNA 突变造成的遗传疾病，都是母系遗传——这点毫无疑问，而且数量非常多。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 大概数十种以上，甚至可能上百种，只是有些还没被发现或报道。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 为什么线粒体一定是妈妈给我们的？因为妈妈的卵细胞里有几万颗、甚至几十万颗线粒体，这么多你知道吗？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 那这个基因呢，就像是我们身体这个调节性T细胞的训练总部。那一旦这个基因一出问题，我们的整个免疫系统的刹车就会失灵。那后来坂口教授他也证实啊，F O X P 3，就是调节性 T 细胞它的开关，那其实只要掌握了它，就可以掌握我们整个免疫平衡的钥匙。那这个发现就让医学界瞬间沸腾了起来啦，因为这个就表示我们也许呢，可以透过这整套的刹车系统治疗我们非常多的疾病。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 那对于这个自体免疫患者来说啊，未来有可能我们可以强化这一群调节性 T 细胞，让他呢去阻止我们身体里的这些“顽劣分子”，阻止这些免疫细胞攻击我们自己的细胞。那对于癌症治疗来说，欸，正好相反喔，因为癌细胞呢比较聪明，它要去贿赂我们这一群调节性 T 细胞，让他疯狂踩刹车，就可以让自己呢躲过免疫系统的攻击。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 所以呢新的免疫疗法呢，反而要让这一群宪兵队去松开他们的刹车，让 T 细胞军团呢好好地放手一搏。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 嗯，灾区其实状况如同大家在新闻画面上看到的那样，就是一片泥泞，然后非常非常混乱，这样子，那现场其实很像是一个大型的RPG的任务，村的那种感觉。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 就是如果我们从志工的角度切进去的话，其实你到了现场，你会被分配到一些任务，但你也可以自己去领任务。每一个人其实在那个当下，你会转换你的身份，就是说，OK，我平常可能是一位老师。那我到现场可能要负责做，比如说动物救援，或者是所谓的铲子超人这些，这样子。对那现场其实因为需要帮助的人跟事情其实非常非常多啦，我们现在举凡所看到的人，基础为生所需要的一些基础建设，一些基本的服务。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 在当地其实都是比较受到影响的，所以你的工作就是需要去协助居民，把一个一个问题解决，然后去把整个灾区的功能恢复。现在有一些地方，比如说像佛祖街那一边，嗯，他的泥巴其实是非常非常厚的，那那个部分其实就是最辛苦的部分。我去的时候其实是9月底的时候，所以那个时间点我们其实都还没有办法真的进到那个佛祖街，但是这几天其实已经可以。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 其实接下来事情更麻烦，因为血液的热量很低，而且营养也不完整，虽然它富含铁质，但是呢？可能太多了诶，也可能会造成我们中毒。那吸血蝙蝠之所以可以靠着血来存活，就是因为它经过数百万年的演化，肠道的结构还有微生物可以帮它们处理血液，这也不是说我们随便喝几口就可以办到的。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 不过科学家告诉我们，虽然我们没办法说不当人类啦，拿起一个死鬼面就让自己变成吸血鬼，但是诶，我们可以透过基因调控的方式来打开我们的一些隐藏能力。那最近一个印度跟丹麦联合的研究，他们在酵母的实验里面就发现。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 有两个特定的基因变异同时存在的时候，就可以唤醒一条身体隐藏的代谢途径。那这条路径呢，就像是按下细胞的潜力开关一样。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 它单独存在的时候其实并不是那么活跃，但它们两个在一起就可以开启我们身体隐藏的能力。那这也告诉我们说，我们的基因之间的互动也许是可以帮我们创造全新的生理功能的。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 首先当然就是说，在伦敦是我们一起来做，其他地方就是不同老师来做。我们的做法其实满简单的，就是说，我们当然先上网去在 Google 地图上查，或者上网去查一些资讯，或者是通过留学生的网络。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 去查到可能哪里有，或者是在我们的受访者告诉我们哪里有跟珍奶有关的店，然后剩下的就是我们在城市里面活动，或者在这个 shopping mall 里看到的这样子。嗯，所以有一些如果是连锁的店，其实是很好的先知道的。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 有一些是额外发现，比如说三明治店，或者是优格店，它也有卖，这个比较是研究者在做田野的过程中间发现的，就偶尔不小心发现。

theme: 田野调查 珍珠奶茶

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 那它表面有非常多覆盖着奇特的湖泊欸，可是这个湖泊里面的液体不是水哦，它里面的液体是液态的甲烷，还有乙烷。那最近 NASA 的一个研究就指出，在这些湖泊里面，有一些简单的分子，它可能会自己自发地组装成一些叫做小泡体的东西。那这些小泡体是什么呢？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 这些小泡体很特别哦，它们有点像我们的细胞一样，会产生双层的膜，里面会包覆着液体，真的就很像是原始细胞的感觉。虽然 NASA 只是先提出这一项可能的发现，其实还没有在土卫六上面看到这些小泡体，但这个发现其实提供了一个全新的假设，就是在极端的环境下，生命的起源或许比我们想象的还要更容易发生。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 当这些液态甲烷的雨打到湖面上的时候，就可能会触发这些分子自发地形成结构，就像在极地的冰雪里，有时候会出现那些长得比较特别的冰晶图案一样。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 那这一项研究呢？也可能在二零二五年八月的 Scientific Reports 大家可以去看一下。那这两个研究也告诉我们一个非常重要的讯息，就是我们比起纠结在蛋白质的来源——我们到底要吃动物的还是植物的——不如呢？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我们应该把注意力放在我们整体的饮食有没有吃得均衡、有无偏差，在某一块。对，同样的道理当然也适用于我们日常生活，还有文化习俗当中。我们其实也不必完全去戒掉我们喜欢的食物，也不用强迫自己吃讨厌的食物。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 其实只要整体我们饮食合理，足够多样，就可以对健康还有长寿带来正面的影响。如果大家对于这一则研究有兴趣，也非常推荐大家可以去读一读我们在 科学快讯 里面的报道，有更多的细节还有专家解析，可以让你更清楚地理解怎么把饮食的策略落实到生活当中。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 好，那我们今天的科学快讯就到这边啦。如果你喜欢我们的节目，也欢迎持续关注，听起来很科学。如果你对于科学的议题有兴趣，也可以到我们的节目说明栏，我们再下面也会把这一次对应的文章放上。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 诶，其实癌症离我们并没有那么遥远。我自己一直觉得“癌”这个字是一个很特别的字，你把它仔细拆开来看，上面有一个“床”，中间有一个“品”，就是三个口，最下面是一个“三”。这个字好像在提醒我们说，癌症其实跟你的三个口脱不了关系，这三个口你可以解读成吃什么、喝什么、洗什么。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 当你吃错、喝错、习惯错，这些东西堆积成山在下面堆积成山之后呢，你就会卧病在床。今天要聊的这个主题呢，就是跟我们的吃跟喝很有关的研究。今天的这个主角呢，不是吸烟，也不是喝酒，而是一个一般存在在肉类、鱼类还有能量饮料里的成分，叫做牛黄酸。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 像很多人熬夜读书加班，或者运动完之后，你可能都会来一杯能量饮料来补一口，觉得它可以帮我们加油打气。实际上，牛黄酸在我们身体里面扮演非常多重要的功能，比如维持我们的视网膜、大脑，还有心脏的正常运作，以及增强运动的表现。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 那再来就是他对于关系的一个互动跟理解可能不是很顺畅。比如说，对方可能说他想要离开，或者可能摆出一个不耐烦的表情，他可能无法理解。这个自闭症的人可能无法理解对方是什么意思，所以总体来说，第一类型就是他在社交沟通和互动上有一种持续性的困难，这是第一个条件要满足。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 第二个就是常常会出现受限或重复性的行为或兴趣。比如说，有些小朋友可能会不断地玩车子，来回重复一个动作，或者他会不断地开关电灯，重复做某一个特定的动作。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 而且他会坚持做这件事情，有时候甚至出现类似仪式性的活动。他们的兴趣有时候会非常狭窄，局限在某一个特定的东西上面。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 所谓的创伤后压力症，它必须是由一个非常巨大的创伤造成的后果。什么叫重大创伤？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 例如自己面临某种危及生死的情况，比如严重车祸，或者重大灾害，像大地震或者911那种非常严重的灾害，自己真的濒临死亡边缘。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 又或者自己目睹了一些濒临死亡的情况，比如亲眼看到一场车祸，或者有人在你面前坠亡之类。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 或者是自己周边非常熟悉的亲友发生了这样的事情，你得知了情况，因为你和他太亲近了，他所面临的那些可怕处境，甚至因此死亡，也会对你造成重大影响。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 这一次研究啊，就是一群科学家，他们找来了一种动物，一群猴子叫做长尾猕猴。那为什么会选这群长尾猕猴？因为长尾猕猴呢有几个蛮特别的特点，第一个是它们是非常看视觉化的动物，视觉其实跟我们人类是非常相似的。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 第二个呢？就是它们也是高度社会化的动物，对于群体关系啊，或者是社交资讯有非常强烈的需求。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 第三个就是啊，猕猴本身呢对于看影片这件事情其实也非常感兴趣，所以呢，科学家呢就找来这些长尾猕猴来做研究，研究它们比较喜欢看怎么样的影片。对这个研究我觉得也还蛮有进入这个搞笑诺贝尔奖的实力的。那科学家是怎么测试这些猕猴的呢？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 那他们就是在荷兰的一个灵长类研究中心里面。这个研究中心里面，本身就有28只圈养的长尾猕猴，它们就在它们的活动空间里面，设置了一个很像小电影院一样的长廊。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 其实这是一个误会误会。因为呢，人类最早发现说这个从牛顿以来发展的物理啊，好像开始不太对劲的时候呢，就说，奇怪啊，怎么在很多的现象里面——特别是物质跟光在交互作用的时候——它吸收的能量有一个最小单位。这在古典物理里面没有这个东西嘛。所以一开始大家以为，哦，这个现象就是新物理最重要的特征。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我们今天讲说你看一个东西在这边，下一个时刻在那边，这个整个过程是连续的啊，可是到了量子物理里面，你就不知道了，你只知道概率。你得到最多的资讯就是概率性的。啊不然我们当然会觉得说概率性的资讯不够好嘛。今天你看电视的这个广播告诉你天气预报百分之七十会下雨，那你说到底会下雨、不会下雨，对不对啊？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 可是其实呢，量子力学就告诉我们说，当你有很多个变量，然后你要做出预测的时候呢，你不可能每一个变量都得到精确准确的预测。那这个其实对我们来讲是一个很大的惊讶的事情啊。对，所以这个可以说是人类知识最大的革命，就发生在上个世纪的二十年代。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 想要知道乐观这个性格的特质，这些乐观的人，大脑到底在想什么？他们的大脑跟其他人的大脑究竟有没有一样呢？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 为了找出这个问题的答案，研究团队他们就设计了两个 fMRI 的实验。fMRI 就是功能性的磁共振影像，这是一种神经影像技术，可以用来测量我们大脑的活动。所以呢，他们就招募了八十七个年龄在二十五岁到四十四岁之间、已婚的成年人。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 然后呢，叫他们来干嘛呢？叫他们来闭上眼睛想想未来，想想未来自己会发生在自己身上的事，或者是发生在伴侣身上的事。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 这些事件呢包含各种不同情绪的情境，像是：你今天即将要出国去玩、去环游世界，你成为了富翁，你得到了时间的自由，你得到了金钱的自由，这种比较正面的情绪。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 还有像是：啊，我可能今天去面试工作、我今天提交履历、我吃个饭，这种比较中性的情绪。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 还有像是：你可能被公司解雇啊，或者你被诊断出——啊，我得了癌症末期——这种比较负面的情绪。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 这些每个事件呢……

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 早期实际上在一九三零年代的时候就有这些想法，他的理论叫做我们后来称为图灵机。那这个机器是怎样呢？它就有个纸带，然后你在纸带这边写 0101，然后让这个纸带喂进这个机器。这个机器会从一个状态——如果多了一个零或一的话——就会跳到另外一个状态。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 然后跳到另外一个状态的话，在这个状态会根据它读到的零、一，就会吐出另外一个零、一的东西出来。所以你有零、一不进去，经过机器你就输出不出来，OK？那这就是我们现在基本的原理。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 图灵他做了一件事：他证明说这个机器是一个 universal machine，也就是说它是一个普及的机器，可以做所有的计算，从这最简单的方式来进行。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: OK，所以我们后来就称它为 Turing machine。那在这个机器的架构下，就有很多科学家开始研究如何在上面去产生一堆新的功能，然后一直在扩大，最后就所谓的计算机理论产生出来。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 第二则呢是非常颠覆认知的一个研究。我们以为不用宝特瓶就比较没有塑胶微粒，但其实不是这样子哦。那我们就从我们的第一则科学快讯开始讲起。不知道大家有没有这样的生活经验：我们走在百货公司啊，或者走在北车火车站，或者是走在动漫展的路上。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 结果呢，原本人流非常顺畅，走着走着，突然间整个大家都挤在一起，前面明明也没有发生什么事，为什么会发生这样奇怪的塞车现象呢？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 现在呢，一个由美国麻省理工大学还有英国巴斯大学合作的研究，就揭露了一个背后的重大秘密。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 人流塞车的秘密其实就在于一个关键角度里面。这个研究呢，他们就先用一个数学模型去计算：如果行人在穿越通道的时候，他们彼此偏离原本的直线路径只要超过十三度，就会出现像刚刚讲到的，变成杂乱无章、大家塞在一起的状态。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 除了这个数学模型之外呢，他们也透过实验去佐证。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 最近有科学家发现，我们看到的东西跟我们的生活环境其实有非常大的关系。对，那今天呢，我们要讲到的这个错觉图，叫做保险箱错觉。保险箱错觉呢，是视觉科学家安东尼在 2006 年年度最佳错觉大赛中发表的一张很著名的图。那它为什么很特别呢？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 因为它是一种叫做双纹带图像的图，就是表示你在看这张图的时候，你的大脑可能会在两种模式之间切来切去。欸你第一次看，你可能会看到的是方形、很多方形，但你再看个几次之后，你会发现，欸怎么又出现了一大堆圆形。最后你会发现，你的大脑每次看到的都不一样，最后你就可以在两个图像之间切换。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 最近就有科学家拿这张图去做一个很特别的研究，他们让不同地方的人去看这张图，结果发现在不同地区的人，他们看到这张图时，第一眼看到的形状是不一样的。

theme: 看到的东西和生活环境

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 目前的科学研究，没有办法给出数据，主要原因是因为这个数据的收集非常困难。我给大家举一个例子哦，比如说通常我们如果想要知道几岁拿到手机是最合适的，那我们应该要跑一个实验嘛。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 比如说，九岁，我们找一些人给他手机；另外十岁找一些人给他手机，十一岁给他手机。就每一组的人，就是在九岁开始、十岁开始、十一岁开始，然后看看到底谁最后的心理状态、这些发展结果是最好的。这个做法就是典型的随机分组。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 但是呢，你想想看，如果你是家长，你会同意吗？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 比如说，如果你是一个家长，不喜欢小孩九岁就有手机，你被随机分到九岁这一组，等于是你的小孩九岁这个科学人员就发手机给他了。那感觉可能就会反对嘛，就是为什么我要这么早给小孩子九岁手机，为什么我要加入这个实验？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 对，所以很多家长可能会不愿意认同这样的实验，因此你无法进行实验收集好。那再来，如果家长同意了，也还有问题。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 那这个灵感其实很简单。如果说蜘蛛丝这么强，坚韧度比刚才高，弹性很好，又能够自然分解，为什么从来都没有人尝试对蜘蛛做基因编辑呢？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 所以研究团队就开始研究了。首先，他们挑选了一些体型迷你、个性不太亲切的大蜘蛛作为实验对象。第一步是先把它的发育眼睛的基因关掉，然后孵出了一群看不到的蜘蛛宝宝，有点残酷，对不对？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 那这证实了这个基因确实关系到视觉发育。然后第二步，他们又把荧光红色的蛋白基因植入到蜘蛛丝的蛋白 M A S P 2 之中。这个蛋白就是用来制作牵引丝的，也就是蜘蛛在天花板上自由摆荡的那条丝的关键成分。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 结果很成功，有些小蜘蛛开始会吐出会发光的红丝了。这也代表它们第一次成功地把外来的基因迁到制造蜘蛛丝的结构里面。预计这种荧光的蜘蛛丝在未来可以再通过更多的改造。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 研究团队就在查理巴黎保护地的一个饮水机床边架了摄像机，要观察它们到底在干嘛。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 他们发现口渴的鹦鹉会有一只脚抓住饮水机，另一只脚扭转去压里面的弹簧，把水流释放出来。它们甚至还懂得要把身体侧倾来施力，再往后仰才能喝到水。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 整个过程其实需要很强的协调能力，但这些鹦鹉几乎都做得到。除了长期追踪特定的个体和它喝水的习惯之外，也发现大概有七成的在地鹦鹉都尝试过这件事情，其中大概有一半会成功。这个行为大概已经可以代表它，实际上已经是当地鹦鹉的日常了。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 艾普林还发现其实不只是喝水，它们甚至也会打开住家垃圾桶的盖子，各种各样的生活技能都非常丰富。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 当然这种定义，并不是真正的所谓的健康。真正的所谓健康，会有更严格的一些筛选程序，确保你是真的很健康。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 可是这种健康可能是正常人群里面的大概前百分之一或是前百分之二的人群才是真正的健康。比如说，他真的每天运动，饮食非常健康，然后他很规律的睡眠，认知功能非常好，这叫做 super ager，就是他是一个超龄的健康人。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 嗯，我们并不是要收这种健康。我们要收的健康是一般的所谓的健康，因为我们最后要跟这个资料库模型去做比对的时候，其实我们就是在比对跟这种我们一般的健康人，我们的脑龄会不会也很健康。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 鱿鱼也会靠手势沟通，其实看到这个研究的时候，我能想到最近看过一个影片：有一个人找他的意大利朋友，把朋友的手绑起来，然后叫他讲话。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 结果发现，他想讲话的时候就想把手拿出来，但手拿不出来，他就不会说话了。说不定以后我们就可以跟这些乌贼用手势交流了，比如说“今天我不会吃你，你赶快逃”。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 挺烂的，好吧。不知道大家有没有想过，如果两只乌贼生气的时候吵架，会是什么样子？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 对，搞不好它们是在打“海带拳”。假设把“海带拳”改良一下，也许我们就可以和友鱼沟通了。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 好，我们的第三则快讯呢，前面都是比较轻松的内容，第三则有一点小难度，是物理领域的一个研究。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 这项研究呢，是来自法国巴黎的高等师范学院，还有意大利理工学院的神经科学团队。他们观察的对象是普通乌贼，也就是英文名字前面带有 common 的最常见的一种乌贼。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 他们发现，这些乌贼会做出一连串动作，它们不是随便乱摆，而是有固定模式的动作。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 第一种动作是把双手往上举，这个动作叫做“上”，很像在呐喊的动作。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 第二个动作是侧身，把它们的八只手都往身体一边卷起来。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 第三个动作叫做“滚”，就是把手臂全部收在头下面，然后凸显出眼睛。有人在文章留言区贴出这个动作，在人类中怎么比喻呢？就是把手放在下巴下面，然后把眼睛睁大，就是一个小花的动作。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 第四个动作叫做“灌”，就是手臂往外展开，像花冠一样。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 接下来我将就选题背景及研究内容、研究过程、研究体会展开答辩。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 大学英文经典原著阅读属于英语通识教育范畴，是湖南科技大学文学文化课程团队紧跟大学英语教学改革步伐所开设的大学英文文化类课程。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 目前国内许多高校都开设了英语原著阅读课，但大多数课程评价体系不完善，或侧重于终结性评价，忽视对学习过程的记录，缺乏相对应的评价标准，从而导致评价不全面。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 在提升学生阅读水平上效果欠佳，也难以评估学生的综合语言运用能力。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 因此，对当前大学生英语原著阅读评价体系进行改革，构建科学高效、适合我国大学生英语原著阅读的评价系统，迫在眉睫。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 今天我非常有幸作为清华万千奋斗者的一员，来讲一讲我自己的科学经历。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 大一我作为曾经的青学竞赛世界冠军，不停的光环压力进入清华，在我的老本行算法竞赛中，尽管我取得了一些成绩，但是当我站在领奖台上，我经常会想：这是我想象的生活吗？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我也偶尔会去工业界实习，但是一旦无法达到我自己真正的兴趣，我在大一的时候，经常在紫仓漫步，思考：我是谁？我要做什么？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 大二我在竞赛和实习之余，一次偶然的机会，我上了一门姚班高年级课程——博弈论。没有想到，这门课程的课程论文竟然成为了我的学术初探。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我在谈判东教授指导下，完成了第一篇学术论文，是关于图灵机和图婚礼结合的问题。完成论文之后，我非常激动，我感觉我的科学兴趣被点燃了，我想要尝试更多的科研方向。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 沈从文本人对于听觉是非常重视的，他认为一切的创作都应该基于官能的感觉。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 他将小说写作的目的定义为叙述感觉或印象，并将文字的作用定义为捕捉感觉与事项。客观来看，这些年来文论界对于听觉文化逐渐重视起来，这也为我们从听觉文化上研究沈从文打开了一个全新的视角。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我的研究综述通过以下三个部分进行梳理：首先是国内外针对文学的声音属性的研究。自从加拿大学者莫里谢弗提出声音景观这一概念，国内学者陆文斌相继开展相关研究，也启发了越来越多的硕士学位论文从事相关研究。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 第二部分是针对沈从文和湘西小说的研究进行了梳理。大家可以看到知网上的第一张发文趋势图，近些年来，沈从文领域研究呈现出研究热情高涨的状态。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 第一张图片是主题主要分布图，可以看出这个领域的研究范围非常广。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 各位同学、老师大家好，我作为最后一个答辩学生，在答辩之前，想先感谢一下我的导师张老师，也谢谢庄老师三年以来的精心培养，同时感谢蔡老师和孙老师在生活上的照顾。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 接下来开始我的正式答辩，我答辩的题目是《高功率激光清洗对钢材表面特性及涂层防护性能的影响机理的研究》。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 激光束的组合清洗就可以，可能清洗差距就更大，这也是一个研究方向。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我的汇报到此结束，恳请各位老师批评指正。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 这是我们最终训练得到的结果，这显示了训练质量的效果。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 这是我们从中抽取的一个房子样本，它的实际价格是二十四点五，而我们用模型预测的是二十三点九七，非常接近。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 接下来是可视化展示。第三部分是逻辑回归与分类问题，前面两部分都是线性回归，输出都是连续值。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 在生活中有很多分类问题，比如手写数字识别，MNIST 数据集，大家可能听过。这个数据集包括七万张由不同人书写的数字图片，并标注了对应数字。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我们训练的最终目的是希望模型能够识别每张图片的数字，并将其分成对应类别。这一章展示了我们对七万张图片进行分类的过程。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 堆积结构具有较好的可控性，这是我们主要的研究方向。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 在镜面仿合材料中，主要包括有机聚合物相和无机相的组合。前者在某些应用领域表现出优异性能，但在结构条件下会受到限制。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 相对而言，无机相不仅具有较高的热稳定性，但机械强度较低。因此我们的研究目标是制备兼具一定强度和性能的复合材料。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 第二部分是研究思路。我们设计的主要方法包括制备工艺和材料微结构调控，其分辨技术可以提高复合材料的切削强度。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我们将通过静电辅助法、材料表面性质调控以及纺织工艺参数优化来改变材料性能。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 在非高温或非高压条件下，材料应保持一定柔性和基本机械强度，同时在必要条件下可以进行相应的断裂处理，以保证材料整体性能。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 那么首先就让我们从故事变法开始，梁启超等人领导的一起资产阶级的改良运动，对于思想界的态度，我们可以从康有为的作品《孔子改制考》中窥见，康有为跟梁启超等人当时正兴致勃勃地准备变法，而就在他们变法的过程当中，他们感受到了一股强大的阻力，为了为变法消除这股阻力，他们开始对一些守旧的传统思想进行了各类抨击，就像这句话中的内容，大概意思就是说这个儒教在很多时候，是关心民众的生活具体到底是如何，而在梁启超等人的论述当中，他们把这种思想更贴近地翻译为西方的一种小资产阶级思想。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 然后根据该疾病的全球版本，研究中国新患者的版本，观察协同，早期检测至关重要，最佳生存率可达诊断高度的百分之九十，然后我将展示第二部分情况，呃，传统诊断方法非常复杂，大量后果，她的简短展示流程涂片被广泛用于海洋筛查，然而所有塑料黑色液体和成本检查率，审查需要时间消耗和工作点，然后我将展示第三种情况，深度学习在医疗领域的应用。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 史铁生的我相信大家还是比较了解，所以简单介绍一下北京的作家无语地坛的描写，地坛公园就在我家边上，离我很近，就一直对这个地方以及这个作家有很深的感情。六九年的时候上山下乡去插队，七零年的时候插队的过程当中得病，七二年回北京，双腿瘫痪，21岁双腿瘫痪，大家可以想象一下什么概念。他的文章里面写的叫做“我在最不好望的年纪，温暖残废的双腿”，在这样一个最近春的年华里面，突然疾病缠身，好像是一个人生最大的悟性。98年的时候，尿毒症靠透析来维持生命。为什么要选《上帝》这个标题，就是因为如果我读完这个人的生平，我觉得不相信他会说出“上帝爱他”这四个字，因为我完全不能理解上帝为什么会爱他，爱他到哪，爱他就给他一双残废的双腿和病痛的身体吧。所以我最开始在读沈从文作品的时候，上帝我其实是在他散文的结尾看到的。最开始的时候，我真的不理解为什么上帝不爱他，我完全不理解，直到我一遍一遍地读，我才理解到世界上对于生命的思考，对于命运的思考是超越绝大多数人的。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 从零六年开始，这三大社交媒体平台巨头就已经开始入局。之后新华社把社交媒体账号统一为“New China”，就完成了N C T V。再之后，一八年国家进行非常重大的国家级国际媒体的经营重组，打造全方位对外传播矩阵，也就是中央广播电视总台成立的那年。新华社也做出了相应的配合，提出要打造网上通讯社，直接面向公众受众实行阵地前移战略。这个事情很好，但我们一直知道在对外宣传或者对外传播上存在很大的问题，这位学者总结了三个问题：宣传烙印依然明显，对新媒体经营管理的重视不足，以及文化与意识形态的差异组合。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 今天我们来看一下《Reinforcement Pretraining》这篇文章。我刚开始看到文章题目的时候，感觉它的想法还是比较有吸引力，也就是用强化学习去做预训练。但实际上看完论文之后，我还是有比较多的疑问。不过本着它的一些创新和新颖的思路，还是给大家分享一下。我们就直接来看吧，读完之后我会在视频最后放一个小小的笔记，总结成省流版。文章的abstract部分介绍了一个叫RPT的方法，也就是Reinforcement Pretraining，这种方法是对大模型和强化学习的一种新的scaling范式，类似于之前的scaling law范式。他的做法是把下一个token的预测作为reasoning task的一个目标，也就是在预测下一个token之前，先做一堆思考的过程，然后再去预测下一个token。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 人的一生当中有很多角色，于我而言，我是一个体育爱好者，一名女篮队员，是杨靖宇和库里的球迷，同样我也是一名新闻学子。而这些角色的融合便是我热爱的源动力。热爱是最好的老师。从小跟爸爸一起打球、看球赛，到后来自己成为女篮队员，对于体育的热爱一直驱动着我。说到篮球，我也经常会去看好朋友们一起打排球，为每一个精彩的扣球喝彩。课余时间，球场是我的常驻地，在重大国际赛事中与朋友云看比赛，也是我们的常态。兴趣是动力，但有一个清晰的自我定位更为重要。为此，我进行了专业的M B T I 测试，经过两个版本的测试，结果均为E N T J统帅型，也就是概念主义者。这个性格在工作中标准高，喜欢提供自由和变化的工作，善于发现事物中的复杂性和可能性。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 青少年的一个特点是叛逆，这意味着你压抑得越多，他们越不服从，你越生气，他们有时就越兴奋。即使青少年自己意识到这段关系不再值得维持，如果你选择坚持，只是为了显示他们对父母的不平衡。此外，如果来自父母的批评比家庭关系更糟糕，可能会让这些青少年更多地依赖他们所爱的人，因为他们从浪漫关系中获得的爱可能是对我们应该从家庭中获得的爱的补偿。这只会加强青少年和他们所爱的人之间的界限，这与父母的期望相反。第三，教导比压抑更重要，北京大学的一项研究表明，这确实与一些负面影响有关。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 其实我们所有人扎根于大地，发源于自然成长与环境，这是一种藕断丝连、生生不息的联系。和谐美丽的环境是我们生命力的源泉，而一根根通天的烟囱、一条条发黑发臭的河流，则会给我们带来不可估量的打击。循着这样的思路，我找到了我最想要去做的事——看万里风光，守一方净土。我将分为三个部分来展开今天的陈述。首先介绍我自己，我是郑州大学生态与环境学院一九级环境工程专业的一名本科生。我喜欢读书、写字、旅行、演讲，而这些爱好的核心是与人沟通——身边的人、老师同学、远方的人。我推崇的东坡，我的偶像米歇尔·奥巴马，都让我感受到生命之美，给我前行的动力与方向。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我们的系统模型共有 N 加一个队列来进行任务的处理。我们记 Q MT 代表第 t 个时间点上 DM 队列上的任务量，而 Q 零 T 代表我们这一台 MEC 服务器上的任务数量。任务的产生符合均值为拉姆达 i 的泊松分布。我们假定每个车辆设备在每个时间片上只有一个任务卸载方向。具体的动态方程是这样的：在第 t 个时间点上，M 队列的任务量与本地可计算的数据量做差。如果能够完全本地计算卸载，那么结果就取零；如果不能，就会产生队列积压。再把这个结果，与第 t 个时间点上新传输到 M 的 DM 队列上的新数据量求和，得到的数值就是下一个时间点 M 队列的总数据量。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 什么是序列转导模型呢？其实它就是一种用来处理序列数据的模型。所谓序列数据，就是指那些具有顺序关系的数据，最典型的像自然语言文本，因为一句话正着说和反着说是不一样的，数据的先后顺序是有意义的。所以像文本翻译、文本生成这些就是很典型的序列转导任务。那么在这篇论文以前，人们是怎么处理这种任务的呢？主流方法是基于复杂的循环神经网络或卷积神经网络，缩写分别是 R N N 和 C N N。当然，绝大多数还是用 R N N，C N N 用得很少，只是在后期作为一种探索。这类方法通常还包括一个编码器和一个解码器。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 这是他一开始就下了一个定义，然后这个是单一的预测。多市场的预测就跟上面说的一样，他觉得多市场存在套利空间。比如市场 X 是 A 队赢或者 B 队赢，市场 Y 是 A 队至少赢两球或者特定得分。如果 Y 的条件成立，也就是 A 队至少赢两球被结算为真，那么在市场 X 里 A 队赢也一定成立，所以定为真。这就是多市场套利，相对于单市场套利。他把这些思路学术化、规范化。也就是说，YES 的代币价值，如果总和不等于一，就存在套利空间。组合套利就是跨市场或者跨条件的套利。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我们具体来看这一项，这是一种价值函数。它包含两项内容。第一项是一个期望，x 是从真实分布中采样得到的，也就是我们游戏里的截图。把 x 放入辨别器并取对数，如果辨别器是完美的，这一项的结果应该等于 1，取对数之后的数值就是 0。第二项是对噪音分布进行采样，把噪音输入生成器生成 x，再把 x 输入辨别器 D。如果辨别器 D 是完美的，这一项的结果也应该等于 0，那么 1 减 0 等于 1，对数 1的结果同样等于 0。所以在辨别器 D 完美的情况下，这两项的结果都等于 0。但如果辨别器 D 不完美，存在误分类的问题，这两项因为对数运算的关系，结果都会变成负数值。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 所以反过来说，如果你做到这一点，这个网络的这个分支其实是共享权重的。这样网络就能够学到一个比较好的表达。后来还有一些有意思的研究，比如预测一个字是不是来自同一个字，例如同一个手写数字六，只是经过了一个细微的旋转。这个工作来源于 Christian 教授，他在德州大学，是一位比较有名的教授，他们的研究很多涉及多模态和自监督。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 下面他说这三个任务是逐渐变得更复杂的。第一个任务是一个匹配任务，相对简单一些；第二个任务需要对匹配上的 caption 进行排序，所以更复杂；第三个任务则是去解释为什么会幽默。三个任务难度逐渐增加。实验中设定了两种场景：一种是使用多模态的 Moi 模块，另一种是使用语言模型。前者是直接把卡通图像输入模型，后者只使用一些关于卡通的描述。最终发现，这两类模型在三个任务上表现都不佳。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: So a few years ago, I did something really brave, or some would say really stupid. I ran for Congress. For years, I had existed safely behind the scenes in politics as a fundraiser, as an organizer, but in my heart, I always wanted to run. The sitting congresswoman had been in my district since nineteen ninety two. She had never lost a race, and no one had really even run against her in a Democratic primary. But in my mind, this was my way to make a difference, to disrupt the status quo. The polls, however, told a very different story. My pollsters told me that I was crazy to run, that there was no way that I could win. But I ran anyway. And in twenty twelve, I became an upstart in a New York City congressional race. I swore I was going to win. I had the endorsement from the New York Daily News, The Wall Street Journal snapped pictures of me on Election Day, and C N B C called it one of the hottest races in the country. I raised money from everyone I knew, including Indian aunties who were just so happy an Indian girl was running. But on Election Day, the polls were right, and I only got nineteen percent of the vote. And the same papers that said I was a rising political star now said I wasted one point three dollars million on six thousand and three hundred and twenty one votes. Don't do the math. It was humiliating. Now, before you get the wrong idea, this is not a talk about the importance of failure, nor is it about leaning in. I tell you the story of how I ran for Congress because I was thirty three years old, and it was the first time in my entire life that I had done something that was truly brave, where I didn't worry about being perfect.

theme: 教女孩子勇敢，而不是完美

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: It is funny the things you forget. I went to see my mother the other day, and she told me this story that I'd completely forgotten about: how when we were driving together, she would pull the car over. And by the time she had got out of the car and gone round to let me out, I would have already got out and pretended to have died. Because that is how you die. And I remember, that was a game I used to play with myself, to entertain myself whenever I was bored or frustrated. Settle down. People say we live in an age of information overload. I don't know about that, but I just know that I get too many marketing emails. I got a marketing email from a supermarket firm, which will remain nameless for predominantly legal reasons, but which I am going to call Safe Mart. I got an email from them, and it went like this: It said, "Just three weeks until Safe Mart at King's Cross opens." And I resented this, because not only do I not remember signing up for that, but I resent the fact that they appear to think that I should be excited about a shop opening. So what I did was I scrolled down to the bottom of the email, and I pressed "Unsubscribe." And I thought that would be the end of it. But a week later, I got another one that said, "Just two weeks until Safe Mart at King's Cross opens." And I thought, well, obviously, I haven't clicked hard enough. Right? I tried it again. Right? Lo and behold, a week passes, you guessed it, "Just one week until Safe Mart at King's Cross opens." And here's the problem: The internet gave us access to everything, but it also gave everything access to us.

theme: 再平淡无聊的生活，也可以变得有趣起来

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Imagine that you are getting ready to go to a party. You feel excited, but also nervous, and you've got this feeling in your stomach, almost like another heartbeat. There is something holding you back, holding you back from getting too happy. No, you mustn't get too happy. Better to be cautious. Otherwise, something bad might happen. You start wondering, who should I talk to when I get there? What if no one wants to talk to me? What if they'll think I am weird? When you arrive at the party, someone comes up to you and starts talking with you, and as this is happening, your mind starts racing. Your heart begins pounding. You start sweating, and it feels almost like you are dissociating from yourself, like it is an out-of-body experience, and you are just watching yourself talk. Keep it together, you say to yourself, but you can't, and it is just getting worse. After a few minutes of conversation, the person you've been speaking to leaves, and you feel utterly defeated. This has been happening to you in social situations for a long time. Or imagine that every time you go out and you are in crowded places, you feel this panic starting to arise. When you are surrounded by lots of people, like on a bus, you start to feel hot, nauseous, uneasy. And to prevent this from happening, you start avoiding a lot of places, which makes you feel lonely and isolated.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Tanya Cushman. Hello. I'd like to introduce you to someone. This is Jomni. That's Jani, but spelled accidentally with an M, in case you were wondering, because we are not all perfect. Jomni is an alien who has been sent to Earth with a mission to study humans. Jomni is feeling lost and alone and far from home, and I think we've all felt this way. Or at least I have. I wrote this story about this alien at a moment in my life when I was feeling particularly alien. I had just moved to Cambridge and started my doctoral program at MIT, and I was feeling intimidated and isolated and very much like I didn't belong. But I had a lifeline of sorts. See, I was writing jokes for years and years and sharing them on social media, and I found that I was turning to doing this more and more. Now, for many people, the internet can feel like a lonely place. It can feel like this, a big, endless, expansive void, where you can constantly call out to it, but no one's ever listening. But I actually found a comfort in speaking out to the void. I found in sharing my feelings with the void, eventually the void started to speak back. And it turns out that the void isn't this endless, lonely expanse at all, but instead it is full of all sorts of other people, also staring out into it and also wanting to be heard. Now, there have been many bad things that have come from social media. I am not trying to dispute that at all. To be online at any given point is to feel so much sadness and anger and violence. It can feel like the end of the world. Yet at the same time, I am conflicted, because I can't deny the fact that so many of my closest friends are people that I had met originally online.

theme: 孤独时，你并不是一个人！

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: It was the spring of twenty eleven, and as they like to say in commencement speeches, I was getting ready to enter the real world. I had recently graduated from college and moved to Paris to start my first job. My dream was to become a war correspondent, but the real world that I found took me into a really different kind of conflict zone. At twenty two years old, I was diagnosed with leukemia. The doctors told me and my parents point blank that I had about a thirty five percent chance of long-term survival. I couldn't wrap my head around what that prognosis meant, but I understood that the reality and the life I'd imagined for myself had shattered. Overnight, I lost my job, my apartment, my independence, and I became patient number five six two four. Over the next four years of chemo, a clinical trial and a bone marrow transplant, the hospital became my home, my bed, the place I lived. Since it was unlikely that I'd ever get better, I had to accept my new reality. And I adapted. I became fluent in medical jargon, made friends with a group of other young cancer patients, built a vast collection of neon wigs, and learned to use my rolling I V pole as a skateboard. I even achieved my dream of becoming a war correspondent, although not in the way I'd expected. It started with a blog reporting from the front lines of my hospital bed, and it morphed into a column I wrote for the New York Times called Life Interrupted.

theme: 死亡教会我活着的意义！

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: I am a highly sensitive person. What's the first thing you think about when I tell you that? That I must be shy and introverted? Or perhaps very emotional? Or maybe even that you need to walk on eggshells around me? The common assumption about highly sensitive people is that we are somehow weak and fragile creatures who picked a losing ticket in the genetic lottery of life. You can see this in action when you Google the word sensitive. You will see images of toothache, irritated skin, wilted dandelions, and crying people. Sensitivity clearly has a PR problem. Today, I want to help change that. Maybe by now you are wondering what it is like to be highly sensitive. I invite you to imagine living with all of your senses on high alert. You also have a vivid inner world where all of your emotions are magnified. Sadness is a deep sorrow, and joy is pure ecstasy. You also care beyond reason and empathize without limits. Imagine being in permanent osmosis with everything around you. Highly sensitive people often hear things like, "You are too sensitive." "Stop taking everything to heart." Or my favorite, "You should really toughen up." The fundamental message is clear. To be highly sensitive is to be highly flawed. I used to agree with that. I always thought I should come with some sort of warning sign or disclaimer: “careful, highly sensitive”

theme: 我喜欢那个敏感的自己

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Thank you so much. I am a journalist. My job is to talk to people from all walks of life all over the world. Today I want to tell you why I decided to do this with my life and what I've learned. My story begins in Caracas, Venezuela, in South America, where I grew up, a place that to me was and always will be filled with magic and wonder. From a very young age, my parents wanted me to have a wider view of the world. I remember one time when I was around seven years old, my dad came up to me and said, "Mariana, I am going to send you and your little sister, who was six at the time, to a place where nobody speaks Spanish. I want you to experience different cultures." He went on and on about the benefits of spending an entire summer in this summer camp in the United States, stressing a little phrase that I didn't pay too much attention to at the time, "You never know what the future holds." Meanwhile, in my seven-year-old mind, I was thinking we were going to get to summer camp in Miami. Maybe it was going to be even better, and we were going to go a little further north to Orlando, where Mickey Mouse lived. I got really excited. My dad, however, had a slightly different plan. From Caracas, he sent us to Brainerd, Minnesota. Mickey Mouse was not up there, and with no cell phone, no Snapchat or Instagram, I couldn't look up any information. We got there, and one of the first things I noticed was that the other kids' hair was several shades of blonde, and most of them had blue eyes.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Letting go can make you unstoppable. I know because I let go of a relationship and reclaimed my life. And I know that letting go can create the best of change for each and every one of you. Let me tell you a story. When I was forty one, the death of a relationship showed me how to truly let go of what wasn't working. See, up until that time, I didn't really think about the future. I kind of lived my life like a dog. Moment to moment, I chased balls, I ate whatever I could find on the ground. And life was good. I had a great job, great friends, a great apartment, a great actual dog, and a great boyfriend. Well, sort of. See, Hector didn't have any skin in the game, and I felt that. He danced around the very idea of marriage, and after tweleve years, we didn't even live together. Still, he gave me hope. Well, sometimes. More like living in a situation that had no hope, just felt normal. Now, don't get me wrong. I stayed because Hector was a good-looking, smart, reliable, and sensitive guy who cared. And while our relationship wasn't perfect, it worked in theory. Until a wake-up call from a friend changed everything. So my realtor friend called to tell me about a condo coming up for sale in my Chicago neighborhood. She knew that I was looking for Hector to commit and thought, well, maybe this could inspire a little forward motion. Still, my first inclination was to say, "We are not ready.”

theme: 学会放手，会让你变得势不可挡

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Are there any other parents here who have struggled to get your kids out the door on time? Yeah, so you know, right? It is like herding kittens. My wife and I would start nagging our three daughters long before it was time to leave, but that obviously wasn't working because we were always late for everything. But one day was a complete gong show. Five minutes before we needed to leave for an important event, I found my oldest daughter on the porch reading, my middle daughter was playing the piano, and my youngest daughter wasn't wearing any socks. So I told them, "Stop reading, stop playing the piano, put on your socks, and everybody get in the car." Five minutes later, nobody was in the car. On my way to help my youngest daughter wear her socks, I noticed my oldest daughter was still on the porch reading. Now I am starting to lose it. Her response: "I didn't hear you." But before I could say a word, I heard the piano start playing again. And that's the story of how I lost my mind. The end. I just wanted my daughters to take a little ownership for getting out the door in time. But then I remembered something I teach management teams: You can't inspire accountability in others until you model it yourself. That's when I realized I wasn't taking any accountability for this problem. I was blaming it totally on my daughters. So I tried a different approach and looked in the mirror. What was I doing or not doing that may be contributing to this problem? Then it hit me. I knew when they needed to be finished with breakfast, dressed, groomed, and ready to leave. But did they? I also knew what time it was. But there were no clocks in their bathrooms. Which I discovered is like a different dimension for my girls where time ceases to exist.

theme: 少抱怨外部因素，多思考如何解决问题！

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Hello, everyone. In South Africa, where I come from, sawobona is the Zulu word for hello. There's a beautiful and powerful intention behind the word, because sawobona literally translated means, "I see you, and by seeing you, I bring you into being." So beautiful. Imagine being greeted like that. But what does it take in the way we see ourselves, our thoughts, our emotions and our stories, that helps us to thrive in an increasingly complex and fraught world? This crucial question has been at the center of my life's work, because how we deal with our inner world drives everything—every aspect of how we love, how we live, how we parent and how we lead. The conventional view of emotions as good or bad, positive or negative, is rigid. And rigidity in the face of complexity is toxic. We need greater levels of emotional agility for true resilience and thriving. My journey with this calling began not in the hallowed halls of a university, but in the messy, tender business of life. I grew up in the white suburbs of apartheid South Africa, a country and community committed to not seeing, to denial. It is denial that makes 50 years of racist legislation possible, while people convince themselves that they are doing nothing wrong. And yet, I first learned of the destructive power of denial at a personal level, before I understood what it was doing to the country of my birth.

theme: 做情绪的主人，而不是情绪的敌人！

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: It was two forty-five pm on a rainy Friday in Los Angeles. My dad was just brewing a cup of coffee in the kitchen when he answered a call from an unknown number. He froze as he heard a woman violently crying and screaming on the other side of the line. Next, a strong masculine voice came on, and he said to my dad, "We have your daughter. And if you don't listen to every single word that we say, we are going to kill her." My dad paused. He lost his breath for a moment, and he managed to ask, "Can I talk to her?" "Do you want us to break her arm?" They taunted him. Now, you have to understand, I am one of two daughters. And sadly, just six months before this phone call came in, we took my big sister off life support. I'll never forget the day that she died. My dad looked at me with this grief, with this heartbreak, bigger than the entire sky. And he kept repeating to me, saying, "Now, I only have one daughter left." So as anyone would when they're in fear, he gave his power away. And he desperately said to the kidnappers, "This is my only daughter. I'll do whatever you want." "Are you alone?" The kidnappers asked him. And in that moment, he locked eyes with my mom across the kitchen, pressed his finger to his lips, silently begging her to remain quiet. And he said to them, "Yep, I am alone," as he scribbled on a napkin. He wrote, "Go outside. Quiet. Call nine one one. Ashley's been kidnapped." We'll be right back. We'll be right back. happens. Hey, we've got one big deal. You say, they just said, you might be right back. We'll be right back. That's right. So right now we are right now. We are right back. We are right back.

theme: 如何知道，你真正想要什么？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: So I want to start by offering you a free no-tech life hack. And all it requires of you is this: that you change your posture for two minutes. But before I give it away, I want to ask you to right now do a little audit of your body and what you are doing with your body. So how many of you are sort of making yourselves smaller? Maybe you are hunching, crossing your legs, maybe wrapping your ankles. Sometimes we hold on to our arms like this. Sometimes we spread out. I see you. So I want you to pay attention to what you are doing right now. We are going to come back to that in a few minutes. And I am hoping that if you sort of learn to tweak this a little bit, it could significantly change the way your life unfolds. So we are really fascinated with body language. And we are particularly interested in other people's body language. You know, we are interested in, like, an awkward interaction or a smile or a contemptuous glance or maybe a very awkward wink or maybe even something like a handshake. Here they are arriving at Number ten, and look at this lucky policeman gets to shake hands with the President of the United States. Oh, and here comes the Prime Minister of the... No. So a handshake or the lack of a handshake can have us talking for weeks and weeks and weeks—even the B B C and the New York Times. So obviously, when we think about nonverbal behavior or body language—but we call it nonverbals as social scientists—it is language. So we think about communication. So we are going to be talking about communication.

theme: 请假装成功 ，你就会真的成功！

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: I've spent nearly two decades observing what makes people luckier than others and trying to help people increase their luck. You see, I teach entrepreneurship, and we all know that most new ventures fail. And innovators and entrepreneurs need all the luck they can get. So what is luck? Luck is defined as success or failure apparently caused by chance. Apparently. That's the operative word. It looks like it is chance, because we rarely see all the levers that come into play to make people lucky. But I've realized by watching so long that luck is rarely a lightning strike, isolated and dramatic. It is much more like the wind blowing constantly. Sometimes it is calm, and sometimes it blows in gusts, and sometimes it comes from directions that you didn't even imagine. So how do you catch the winds of luck? It is easy, but it is not obvious. So I am going to share some things—three things with you—that you can do to build a sail to capture the winds of luck. The first thing you want to do is to change your relationship with yourself. Be willing to take small risks that get you out of your comfort zone. Now, when we are children, we do this all the time. We have to do this if we are going to learn how to walk or talk or ride a bike, or even quantum mechanics, right? We need to go from someone who doesn't ride a bike one week to someone who does the next. And this requires us to get out of our comfort zone and take some risks.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: If your life were a book, and you were the author, how would you want your story to go? That's the question that changed my life forever. Growing up in the hot Las Vegas desert, all I wanted was to be free. I would daydream about traveling the world, living in a place where it snowed, and I would picture all of the stories that I would go on to tell. At the age of 19, the day after I graduated high school, I moved to a place where it snowed, and I became a massage therapist. With this job, all I needed were my hands and my massage table by my side, and I could go anywhere. For the first time in my life, I felt free, independent, and completely in control of my life. That is, until my life took a detour. I went home from work early one day with what I thought was the flu, and less than twenty four hours later, I was in the hospital, on life support, with less than a two percent chance of living. It wasn't until days later, as I lay in a coma, that the doctors diagnosed me with bacterial meningitis, a vaccine-preventable blood infection. Over the course of two and a half months, I lost my spleen, my kidneys, the hearing in my left ear, and both of my legs below the knee. When my parents wheeled me out of the hospital, I felt like I had been pieced back together like a patchwork doll.

theme: 再艰难，你也要跳出舒适区去生活

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: When people find out I write about time management, they assume two things. One is that I am always on time. And I am not. I have four small children, and I would like to blame them for my occasional tardiness, but sometimes it is just not their fault. I was once late to my own speech on time management. We all had to just take a moment together and savor that irony. The second thing they assume is that I have lots of tips and tricks for saving bits of time here and there. And sometimes I'll hear from magazines that are doing a story along these lines, generally on how to help their readers find an extra hour in the day. And the idea is that we'll shave bits of time off every day activities, add it up, and we'll have time for the good stuff. And I question the entire premise of this piece, but I am always interested in hearing what they've come up with before they call me. So some of my favorites. Doing errands in a way where you only have to make right-hand turns. Being extremely judicious in microwave usage, so it says three to three and a half minutes on the package. We are totally getting in on the bottom side of that. And my personal favorite, which makes sense on some level, is to D V R your favorite shows, so you can fast-forward through the commercials. And that way you save about eight minutes every half hour. So in the course of two hours of watching TV, you find thirty two minutes to exercise. Which is true. You know another way to find 32 minutes to exercise? Don't watch two hours of TV a day, right? Anyway, the idea is we'll save bits of time here and there, add it up, we will finally get to everything we want to do. But after studying how successful people spend their time, and looking at their schedules hour by hour, I think this idea has it completely backward. We don't build the lives we want by saving time. We build the lives we want, and then time saves itself.

theme: 如何掌控你的自由时间

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: One of my earliest memories is of trying to wake up one of my relatives and not being able to. And I was just a little kid, so I didn't really understand why. But as I got older, I realized we had drug addiction in my family, including later cocaine addiction. I've been thinking about it a lot lately, partly because it is now exactly 100 years since drugs were first banned in the United States and Britain, and we then imposed that on the rest of the world. It is a century since we made this really fateful decision to take addicts and punish them and make them suffer because we believed that would deter them, it would give them an incentive to stop. And a few years ago, I was looking at some of the addicts in my life who I love and trying to figure out if there was some way to help them. And I realized there were loads of incredibly basic questions I just didn't know the answer to. Like, what really causes addiction? Why do we carry on with this approach that doesn't seem to be working? And is there a better way out there that we could try instead? So I read loads of stuff about it, and I couldn't really find the answers I was looking for. So I thought, okay, I'll go and sit with different people around the world who've lived this and studied this and talk to them and see if I can learn from them. And I ended up, I didn't realize I would end up going over thirty thousand miles at the start, but I ended up going and meeting loads of different people, from a transgender crack dealer in Brownsville, Brooklyn, to a scientist who spends a lot of time feeding hallucinogens to mongooses to see if they like them. It turns out they do, but only in very specific circumstances. To the only country that's ever decriminalized all drugs, from cannabis to crack, Portugal. And the thing I realized that really blew my mind is, almost everything we think we know about addiction is wrong. And if we start to absorb the new evidence about addiction, I think we are going to have to change a lot more than our drug policies. But let's start with what we think we know, what I thought I knew, right? Let's think about this middle row here, right? Imagine all of you, for 20 days now, went off and used heroin three times a day. Some of you look a little bit more enthusiastic than others at this prospect.

theme: 当你一直沉迷于"垃圾快乐"时，自律和自虐也救不了你

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Hi, everyone. Two years ago, my life changed forever. My wife, Kelsey, and I welcomed our daughter, Leela, into the world. Now, becoming a parent is an amazing, amazing experience. Your whole world changes overnight, and all of your priorities change immediately, so fast that it makes it really difficult to process sometimes. Now, you also have to learn a tremendous amount about being a parent. Like, for example, how to dress your child. This was new to me. This is an actual outfit. I thought this was a good idea. And even Leela knows that it is not a good idea. So there was so much to learn and so much craziness all at once. And to add to the craziness, Kelsey and I both work from home. We are entrepreneurs. We run our own businesses. So Kelsey develops courses online for yoga teachers. I am an author. And so I am working from home. Kelsey's working from home. We have an infant. And we are trying to make sure that everything gets done that needs to be done. And life is really, really, really busy. And a couple weeks into this amazing experience, when the sleep deprivation really kicked in, like around week eight, I had this thought, and it was the same thought that parents across the ages, internationally, everybody has had this thought, which is, I am never going to have free time.

theme: 别不信，你只需20个小时，就能学会任何事情

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: So, I'll start with this. A couple of years ago, an event planner called me because I was going to do a speaking event. And she called and she said, "I am really struggling with how to write about you on the little flyer." And I thought, well, what's the struggle? And she said, "Well, I saw you speak and I am going to call you a researcher, I think, but I am afraid if I call you a researcher, no one will come because they'll think you are boring and irrelevant." And I was like, okay. And she said, "But the thing I liked about your talk is you are a storyteller, so I think what I'll do is just call you a storyteller." And of course, the academic, insecure part of me was like, "you are going to call me a what?" And she said, "I am going to call you a storyteller." And I was like, "Why not magic pixie?" I was like, "I don't, let me think about this for a second." And so, I tried to call deep on my courage. And I thought, you know, I am a storyteller. I am a qualitative researcher. I collect stories. That's what I do. And maybe stories are just data with a soul, you know, and maybe I am just a storyteller. So, I said, "You know what? Why don't you just say I am a researcher storyteller?" And she went, "There's no such thing." So, I am a researcher storyteller. And I am going to talk to you today. We are talking about expanding perception. And so, I want to talk to you and tell some stories about a piece of my research that fundamentally expanded my perception and really actually changed the way that I live and love and work and parent. And this is where my story starts.

theme: 真正的强大，是敢于面对那个脆弱、不完美的自己

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Gopal. When I was 27 years old, I left a very demanding job in management consulting for a job that was even more demanding, teaching. I went to teach 7th graders math in the New York City public schools. And like any teacher, I made quizzes and tests. I gave out homework assignments. When the work came back, I calculated grades. What struck me was that IQ was not the only difference between my best and my worst students. Some of my strongest performers did not have stratospheric IQ scores. Some of my smartest kids weren't doing so well. And that got me thinking. The kinds of things you need to learn in 7th grade math, sure, they're hard. Ratios, decimals, the area of a parallelogram. But these concepts are not impossible. And I was firmly convinced that every one of my students could learn the material if they worked hard and long enough. After several more years of teaching, I came to the conclusion that what we need in education is a much better understanding of students and learning from a motivational perspective, from a psychological perspective. In education, the one thing we know how to measure best is IQ. But what if doing well in school and in life depends on much more than your ability to learn quickly and easily? So I left the classroom, and I went to graduate school to become a psychologist. And there were a lot of real students who got me to do that. So I went to graduate school. I was an adult in life, and I got to see my first question there, and I've been here in the same class, wishing that to even learn how to measure headed and aviation countless pencement.

theme: 学习能力是可以通过努力改变的0-1:54

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: I must say, this is my first time in Taiwan, actually my first time in Asia. I am really excited. Thank you. I've only been here for two days, but... Before I speak, I've got something important to say. This is TED, so they told me to be very brief and to the point, concise. So I promise I will be as brief as possible, no matter how long it takes. I come from a conference that was held in Doha, Qatar, at the International Education and Resource Network Summit, one of the most important conferences in the world about education. And I've done it a couple of other times. So, right, successful, running around the world, everything was laid out for me. Not. And I come from a small village in the north of Italy, from a lower middle class family. Actually, to be perfectly honest, to European standards, a pretty poor family. And if I look back in my past, I realize that very few would have bet on me. I was, you know, one of the losers. I was kind of a nerd. I am proud of it. It was fun. Still am, so... But, I mean, the school environment was really tough. You had to fight your way out. When I say fight, I don't mean in metaphorical terms. I mean literally fight.

theme: 真正拉开你与周围人之间差距的，是自学能力

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Hi everybody. Anaya, thank you for that beautiful introduction. I could not be prouder of everything you've done in your time with the Obama Foundation. And of course, I couldn't be prouder of all of you in the graduating class of twenty twenty, as well as the teachers and the coaches, and most of all, parents and family who guided you along the way. Now, graduating is a big achievement under any circumstances. Some of you have had to overcome serious obstacles along the way, whether it was an illness, or a parent losing a job, or living in a neighborhood where people too often count you out. Along with the usual challenges of growing up, all of you have had to deal with the added pressures of social media, reports of school shootings, and the specter of climate change. And then, just as you are about to celebrate having made it through, just as you've been looking forward to proms and senior nights, graduation ceremonies, and let's face it, a whole bunch of parties, the world is turned upside down by a global pandemic. And as much as I am sure you love your parents, I'll bet that being stuck at home with them, and playing board games or watching Tiger King on TV is not exactly how you envisioned the last few months of your senior year. Now I'll be honest with you, the disappointments of missing a live graduation, those will pass pretty quickly. I don't remember much of my own high school graduation. I know that not having to sit there and listen to a commencement speaker isn't all that bad. Mine usually go on way too long. Also, not that many people look great in those caps. Especially if you have big ears like me. And you'll have plenty of time to catch up with your friends once the immediate public health crisis is over. But what remains true is that your graduation marks your passage into adulthood.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: The first of which is, life can be heavy, especially if you try to carry it all at once. Part of growing up and moving into new chapters of your life is about catch and release. What I mean by that is, knowing what things to keep and what things to release. You can't carry all things. Decide what is yours to hold. Let the rest go. Often times, the good things in your life are lighter anyway, so there's more room for them. One toxic relationship can outweigh so many wonderful, simple joys. You get to pick what your life has time and room for. Be discerning. Secondly, learn to live alongside cringe. No matter how hard you try to avoid being cringe, you will look back on your life and cringe retrospectively. Cringe is unavoidable over a lifetime. Even the term cringe might someday be deemed cringe. I promise you, you are probably doing or wearing something right now that you will look back on later and find revolting and hilarious. You can't avoid it, so don't try to. For example, I had a phase where, for the entirety of twenty twelve, I dressed like a nineteen fifties housewife. But you know what? I was having fun. Trends and phases are fun. Looking back and laughing is fun. And while we are talking about things that make us squirm but really shouldn't, I'd like to say I am a big advocate for not hiding your enthusiasm for things.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Thank you, Alina, for such a generous introduction. And on behalf of the entire He For She team, I also want to thank UNESCO for all of its support. It is meant a lot to us. Thank you all for being here for this important moment. These men from all over the world have decided to make gender equality a priority in their lives and in their universities. Thank you for making this commitment. I graduated from university four years ago. I had always dreamed of going, and I know how fortunate I am to have had the opportunity to do so. Brown became my home, my community, and I took the ideas and the experiences I had there into all of my social interactions, into my workplace, into my politics, into all aspects of my life. I know that my university experience shaped who I am. And of course, it does for many people. But what if our experience of university shows us that women don't belong in leadership? What if it shows us that, yes, women can study, but they shouldn't lead a seminar? What if, as still in many places around the world, it tells us that women don't belong there at all? What if, as is the case in far too many universities, we are given the message that sexual violence isn't actually a form of violence? But we know that if you change students' experiences so they have different expectations of the world around them, expectations of equality, society will change. As we leave home for the first time to study at the places that we have worked so hard to get, we must not see or experience double standards. But we are going to do that in place.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: When I was a very young person, I began my career as an actress. Whenever my mother wasn't free to drive me into Manhattan for auditions, I would take the train from suburban New Jersey and meet my father, who would have left his desk at the law office where he worked, and we would meet under the upper platform arrival and departure sign in Penn Station. We would then get on the subway together, and when we surfaced, he would ask me, which way is north? I wasn't very good at finding north in the beginning, but I auditioned a fair amount, and so my dad kept asking me, which way is north? Over time, I got better at finding it. I was struck by that memory yesterday while boarding the plane to come here, not just by how far my life has come since then, but by how meaningful that seemingly small lesson has been. When I was still a child, my father developed my sense of direction, and now, as an adult, I trust my ability to navigate space. My father helped give me the confidence to guide myself through the world. In late March last year, twenty sixteen, I became a parent for the first time. I remember the indescribable and, as I understand it, pretty universal experience of holding my weak old son and feeling my priorities change on a cellular level. I remember I experienced a shift in consciousness that gave me the ability to maintain my love of career and also cherish something else, someone else, so much, much more. Like so many parents, I wondered how I was going to balance my work with my new role as parent, and in that moment, I remember that the statistic for the US's policy on maternity leave flashed in my mind. American women are currently entitled to twelve weeks unpaid leave. American men are entitled to nothing. American men are entitled to nothing.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: I stand before you not as an expert, but as a concerned citizen. One of the four thousand thousand people who marched in the streets of New York on Sunday, and the billions of others around the world who want to solve our climate crisis. As an actor, I pretend for a living. I play fictitious characters, often solving fictitious problems. I believe that mankind has looked at climate change in that same way, as if it were a fiction, as if pretending that climate change wasn't real would somehow make it go away. But I think we all know better than that now. Every week we are seeing new and undeniable climate events, evidence that accelerated climate change is here right now. Our oceans are acidifying, with methane plumes rising up from the ocean floor. We are seeing extreme weather events, and the West Antarctic and Greenland ice sheets melting at unprecedented rates, decades ahead of scientific projections. None of this is rhetoric, and none of it is hysteria. It is fact. The scientific community knows it. Industry knows it. Governments know it. Even the United States military knows it. The chief of the US Navy's Pacific Command, Admiral Samuel Locklear, recently said that climate change is our single greatest security threat. My friends, this body, perhaps more than any other gathering in human history, now faces this difficult but achievable task. You can make history, or you will be vilified by it. To be clear, this is not about just telling people to change their light bulbs or to buy a hybrid car. This disaster has grown beyond the choices that individuals make.
